preamble: The denasalization of nasalized vowels, particularly front ones, is a common phonetic transformation. This process often involves the loss of nasal airflow, which can be accompanied by a lowering of the vowel's height, leading to a more open articulation.

examples.note: In the historical phonology of French, nasal vowels underwent a process of lowering and denasalization. A close-mid front nasal vowel /ẽ/, if present at an earlier stage, would have tended to lower to an open-mid front nasal vowel /ɛ̃/. Subsequently, in contexts where the nasal consonant was lost, this /ɛ̃/ could denasalize to an oral open-mid front vowel /ɛ/. This reflects a general tendency for nasal vowels to lower upon denasalization.

sources: Kennedy Terry, K., & Russell, E. (n.d.). Modeling the emergence of a typological anomaly: Vowel nasalization in French. Proceedings.
